published.text:
### Hero
Heading: AI Appointment Setter vs. Virtual Assistant
Body: If your main problem is missed calls, slow lead response, and inconsistent booking follow-up, an AI appointment setter usually solves it faster and cheaper than hiring a virtual assistant to watch the inbox. If your main problem is broader admin work — reschedules, inbox cleanup, document chasing, CRM cleanup, and one-off tasks — a virtual assistant may still be the better first hire. This guide compares both options honestly, with a focus on the real tradeoff: instant automated coverage versus flexible human admin support.
Sub: Below: where AI wins, where a human VA still wins, and why many service businesses end up using AI for first response and a VA for exception handling instead of forcing one tool to do both jobs.

### Core sections
- Two different ways to fix the same booking bottleneck
- Side-by-side comparison
- When each option usually wins
- What most businesses miss in this comparison
- Relevant proof from adjacent builds

### FAQ
Subhead: Practical questions from businesses deciding between instant automation and flexible admin help
- Is an AI appointment setter better than hiring a part-time VA?
- Can I use AI first and add a VA later?
- What kinds of tasks should stay with a human VA?
- Will AI hurt the customer experience compared with a human assistant?
- What is the simplest way to decide between the two?

### Final CTA
Heading: Not sure whether you need AI, a VA, or both?
Body: Book a 30-minute call. We will map your current lead flow, identify where response delay or admin drag is costing you deals, and recommend the simplest setup that fixes the actual bottleneck.

edits.description: Chosen because backlog reconciliation showed this was the last distinct planned comparison still worth shipping, provided it was framed around VA/admin handoff economics instead of duplicating receptionist or call-center intent.
Kept the page tightly bounded to speed-to-lead versus admin-flexibility tradeoffs, with hybrid guidance as the practical middle ground.
Used adjacent proof only: after-hours call coverage, booking automation, and CRM automation from existing case studies rather than inventing VA-specific client claims.
Tone stays direct, non-hype, and explicitly fit-based instead of pushing AI as a universal replacement.
